Client 2: Yeah, and the monthly stuff feels tight even though on paper we’re fine.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll map the accounts and the mortgage and insurance. Income first—what are you two bringing in gross per year as a household?
Client 1: Household gross is 126800 a year.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And is that mostly salary, or is there any business income mixed in?
Client 2: It’s business income, paid monthly, but it totals 126800 annually.

Client 2: It’s around ten grand a month—give or take.
Advisor: Totally fair. If you had to pin it to the number you track—what’s the monthly total you’re using?
Client 1: The total monthly expenses number we use is 10050.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just noting that down. Within that, how much is going to debt payments each month in total?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one. Monthly debt payments total is 4800.

Client 1: The total monthly expenses number we use is 10050.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just noting that down. Within that, how much is going to debt payments each month in total?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one. Monthly debt payments total is 4800.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms—4800 is the monthly payment, not the remaining balance, right?
Client 2: Right—payment. The balance is separate.

Client 2: We’ve been using 153100 for the property value.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to investable assets—retirement and brokerage-type money—about how much do you have total across accounts?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 296300.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that down so I understand what’s where. Elizabeth, what’s the largest account you’re thinking of?
Client 1: I have a taxable account held at an insurance company—value is 200600.

Client 1: Balance outstanding is 112050, interest rate is 4.38%, and the final payment date is 2047-03-23.
Advisor: Got it. And the home value you’re using—what do you estimate the property is worth today?
Client 2: We’ve been using 153100 for the property value.

Client 2: Right—payment. The balance is separate.

Client 2: Yeah, and the monthly stuff feels tight even though on paper we’re fine.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll map the accounts and the mortgage and insurance. Income first—what are you two bringing in gross per year as a household?
Client 1: Household gross is 126800 a year.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And is that mostly salary, or is there any business income mixed in?
Client 2: It’s business income, paid monthly, but it totals 126800 annually.

Client 2: I always mix this up—like, is that the same as what comes out of my paycheck?
Advisor: Close, but not exactly. Withholding is what comes out; the bracket is where your taxable income lands at the end of the year. Do you remember the bracket percentage you typically use as your planning assumption?
Client 1: Yes—32%. Tax bracket band is 32%.
Advisor: Perfect, that’s what I needed.
Advisor: Alright—so just to connect the dots with what you said earlier: you’ve got the mortgage as the only debt, life insurance on you, Elizabeth, and then a mix of retirement and taxable investing. Next I’ll ask about priorities for the kids—college, childcare, that kind of thing. Sound okay?

Advisor: Got it. And when you say “more invested,” Elizabeth, is that mainly retirement accounts, or more in the taxable side too?
Client 1: Both. I want retirement moving, but I also like having taxable money we can access if we need it.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Let me zoom out for two quick household basics so I’m not making assumptions—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Thanks. And kids—how many dependents are we talking about that you’re supporting day to day?

Advisor: Okay. And retirement accounts—do you have a 401(k) or IRA bucket as well?
Client 1: Yes—there’s one held at a bank, it’s my 401k/IRA bucket, and it’s 51750.
Advisor: And any other retirement platform account—like a smaller retirement account elsewhere?
Client 1: There’s another one held at a retirement platform, value 21800.
Advisor: Any private investments—private markets, that kind of thing?

Client 1: The total monthly expenses number we use is 10050.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just noting that down. Within that, how much is going to debt payments each month in total?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one. Monthly debt payments total is 4800.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms—4800 is the monthly payment, not the remaining balance, right?
Client 2: Right—payment. The balance is separate.

Advisor: Hi Elizabeth, hi Thomas—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi. Honestly, we just want a real plan—like, we’re doing a lot but it doesn’t feel coordinated.
Client 2: Yeah, and the monthly stuff feels tight even though on paper we’re fine.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll map the accounts and the mortgage and insurance. Income first—what are you two bringing in gross per year as a household?

Advisor: Perfect. And household-wise, it’s just you two adults in the home—so two adults total?
Client 2: Yeah, two adults—2.
Advisor: Got it. And just so you know how I work with clients—my firm is an RIA, a registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory relationship you’re expecting here, or were you looking for something more like product-only?
Client 1: No, that’s what we want—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay, thanks. Now, risk—when we build the investment plan, do you want it set up as conservative, moderate, or aggressive overall?

Advisor: Hi Elizabeth, hi Thomas—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi. Honestly, we just want a real plan—like, we’re doing a lot but it doesn’t feel coordinated.
Client 2: Yeah, and the monthly stuff feels tight even though on paper we’re fine.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll map the accounts and the mortgage and insurance. Income first—what are you two bringing in gross per year as a household?
Client 1: Household gross is 126800 a year.

Advisor: Perfect. That’s a helpful baseline. Before we wrap this section—anything else major debt besides the mortgage?
Client 2: No, just the mortgage.
Advisor: Okay. So we’ve got income at 126800 gross annually, spending at 10050 a month, the mortgage payment at 4800 monthly, and investable assets at 296300 total. Next I’ll ask a few questions about goals and how aggressive you want to be with investing—sound good?
Client 1: Yep, let’s do it.
Client 2: Sounds good.

Advisor: On income, you’ve got business income coming in monthly, and the annual total you’re using is 126,800. Still the right way to think about it?
Client 1: Yes, that’s right—126,800 annually, paid monthly.
Advisor: Okay. And you’re both employed as well—Elizabeth at 87,300 gross annually and Thomas at 39,500 gross annually, correct?
Client 2: Yep—mine is 39,500.
Client 1: And mine is 87,300.

Client 1: Yes—coverage amount is 590,400, covered until 2051-09-30.
Advisor: Thanks. That’s the core snapshot. Now, with four dependents, what’s the biggest kid-related cost right now—childcare, activities, or are you already thinking college savings?
Advisor: Got it. Before we dig into kid costs, I want to make sure I’m not mixing up who’s who—Thomas, you’re the spouse/partner in the household, right?
Client 2: Yeah, I’m the spouse/partner.
Advisor: Thanks, Thomas. And just to confirm the labeling we’re using in the plan—are you listed as Client 2?
